lessonTitle: Student Loans — How They Work Across Different Regions
concept: Not all student loans work the same way — where you study determines whether your loan is a manageable tool or a lifelong burden.

hook.fact: Two graduates. Both borrowed $30,000. One repaid it in 5 years. One is still repaying 20 years later.
hook.question: Same amount. Very different loan systems.

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: A **student loan** lets you attend university now and repay later. The cost of borrowing is interest — a percentage charged annually on your outstanding balance. Different loan systems charge very different rates and have very different repayment rules.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: In the **US**, federal student loans (available via FAFSA, US citizens only) charge fixed interest rates of 5–8% and offer income-driven repayment plans. Private loans (from banks) carry higher rates and fewer protections. International students in the US typically cannot

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: In the **UK**, Student Finance England charges a Plan 5 interest rate (RPI + up to 3%, currently around 7.1%) but uses **income-contingent repayment**: you only repay 9% of income above £25,000/year. If you never earn enough, unpaid loans are

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: **Income-contingent repayment** means your monthly payment is tied to what you earn, not what you borrowed. A UK graduate earning £28,000 repays just £270/year — regardless of their total loan balance. This protects low earners from unmanageable debt.

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: In the **EU**, student loan availability varies dramatically by country. Germany, France, and the Nordics have low or no tuition, so most students do not need large loans. Some EU countries offer state-backed student loans; many do not. Family funding

conceptCards.title: Card 6
conceptCards.body: In **East Asia**, formal public student loan systems are limited. Japan's JASSO loan programme is the main exception, with interest-free options for eligible students. In Singapore, China, South Korea, and Hong Kong, family funding is typically the primary model —

conceptCards.title: Card 7
conceptCards.body: Now test this: The most dangerous borrowing is **private student loans** (especially in the US) and consumer credit used as a substitute for a proper student loan. These carry high interest rates, no income-contingent protections, and can create debt that

options: You repay based on your total loan balance
Your monthly repayment is a fixed percentage of income above a threshold
You only repay if you earn above the median salary
The government repays part of your loan for you
questions.explanation: "Income-contingent means your repayment scales with your earnings — if you earn less, you pay less. The UK system uses this model."

questions.statement: A UK graduate who earns £22,000/year after graduation would make significant monthly repayments on their student loan.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "UK income-contingent repayment only kicks in above £25,000. At £22,000, the graduate repays nothing — regardless of their loan balance."

questions.prompt: Match each loan system to its region:

pairs.right: Much of East Asia and parts of EU

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "In the UK, if a graduate never earns enough to repay their full student loan, the remaining balance is written off after ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: A UK graduate earns £32,000. They repay 9% of income above £25,000.

questions.sentence: Complete: "In the UK, a graduate earning below £25,000 repays ___ on their student loan. A graduate earning £35,000 repays 9% of ___, which equals ___ per year."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Marcus is planning to study in the US as an international student. His plan: "I'll just take out federal student loans to cover my costs."
options: Federal loans have high interest rates
International students are ineligible for US federal student loans
Federal loans require repayment while studying
questions.explanation: "FAFSA federal loans are available to US citizens and eligible non-citizens only. International students must use private loans (higher rates, fewer protections) or other funding sources."

tasks.scenario: Kai is choosing between studying in the UK (borrowing £40,000 in student loans) and the US (borrowing $60,000 in private loans at 9% APR). He expects to earn £35,000/$45,000 in his first job.
tasks.prompt: Which loan is more manageable given his expected income?
tasks.explanation: "UK: 9% × (£35,000 − £25,000) = £900/year. US: $60,000 at 9% APR accrues $5,400 in interest in Year 1 alone — on a $45,000 salary, this is immediately painful. The income-contingent system makes the UK loan far more manageable."

tasks.scenario: Sofia borrows $50,000 in US private loans at 8% APR. She does not make payments during her 4-year degree (interest capitalises annually).
tasks.prompt: What is the approximate balance at graduation?
tasks.explanation: "$50,000 × (1.08)^4 = $68,024. Four years of 8% compounding adds $18,024 to her balance before she earns a dollar. This is why private loans require very careful evaluation."

lockIn.takeaway: The region you study in determines how your loan works — income-contingent systems protect low earners; private loans do not.

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Income-contingent repayment anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Income-contingent repayment include beyond tuition?
